name: documentation-api
description: Practices for developer / API documentation: contracts, generated reference, examples, and deprecation policy

decisions.decision: The API is defined by a schema document (OpenAPI for REST, AsyncAPI for events, GraphQL SDL, protobuf for gRPC) before the implementation, and the schema is the source of truth
decisions.rationale: A schema defined after the code documents what happened to ship. A schema defined first is a contract that clients, servers, and tooling all agree on — and catches design problems before they are load-bearing.
decisions.details: Keep the schema in the repo, review it in PRs, and generate clients, servers, validation, and docs from it. Hand-written schemas drift less than hand-written documentation prose. For events, AsyncAPI plays the same role as OpenAPI for requests. Choose hand-authored or code-generated schema depending on team workflow — but pick one side as source of truth, not both.

decisions.decision: Reference documentation is generated from the schema and in-code documentation comments — never hand-written and maintained in parallel
decisions.rationale: Hand-maintained reference docs diverge from the code within weeks. Generated reference stays accurate by construction because the build breaks if it does not.
decisions.details: For OpenAPI / AsyncAPI schemas, render with Redoc, Swagger UI, Scalar, or Stoplight Elements. For code-level APIs, use the language's native tooling: godoc, rustdoc, typedoc, pdoc, dartdoc, javadoc. Publish the rendered reference alongside end-user docs. Humans write guides, tutorials, and conceptual docs — machines generate the reference.

decisions.decision: API examples shown in documentation are executable — doctests, integration-test-backed snippets, or runnable sandboxes — so they cannot silently rot
decisions.rationale: An API example that no test exercises is a hostage to the next refactor. Once broken it stays broken until the next bug report, and every bug report costs more than wiring up the test once.
decisions.details: Use the language's native doctest mechanism where it exists (Rust doctests, Python doctest, Go testable examples). For prose guides, extract the example code into a tested fixture and inject it into the docs at build time, or link the guide to the integration test that exercises the same flow. Interactive sandboxes (e.g. embedded playgrounds) are a strong alternative when the API is stateless.

decisions.decision: The project publishes an explicit deprecation policy: notice period, migration path, sunset date, and how it is communicated
decisions.rationale: Consumers of an API plan around stability. An unannounced removal — or a 'deprecated since forever' notice that never becomes a removal — both damage trust. A written policy lets consumers estimate their own work.
decisions.details: Cover: how deprecations are announced (CHANGELOG, release notes, in-response headers, runtime warnings), the minimum notice period before removal, what a migration guide must contain, and how the project signals 'deprecated' in the schema (OpenAPI deprecated flag, GraphQL @deprecated directive, protobuf reserved fields). Ship a migration guide alongside each deprecation, not at removal time.

decisions.decision: API-surface changes are recorded in an API-specific changelog distinct from the user changelog — with breaking changes, schema diffs, and migration notes
decisions.rationale: A user reading 'what's new' wants feature highlights. An API consumer reading 'what changed in the API' wants a mechanical diff of endpoints, fields, and behaviours. One document cannot serve both audiences well.
decisions.details: Keep an API changelog scoped to the API surface: added / changed / removed endpoints or events, field changes, breaking behaviour, and migration steps. For machine-readable diffs, tools like oasdiff, openapi-diff, or graphql-inspector can generate schema deltas between versions that the changelog cites. Version the API changelog alongside the API itself.
